items.rule: Open on a real signature skewer leaving the grill, being seasoned, or getting the first bite. Do not lead with empty decor or neon ambience.
items.why_it_matters: A barbecue shop earns trust from visible heat, grill work, and real dine-in proof. If viewers only see mood shots first, the store feels generic and unverified.
execution_points: Make sure the first 3 shots include grill action, a plated or handed-over skewer, and a bite or table interaction.
Keep the cover direction aligned with the first hero item. If the hook is lamb skewers, show lamb skewers first.
Even for late-night or social scripts, start from food proof before space or nightlife mood.
items.fallback_route: If peak-hour dine-in footage is missing, downgrade to a signature-skewer explainer, a single-table tasting route, or a prep-to-plate route.
must_avoid: long empty-store opening sequences
external food stock used as store proof
cold static plates framed as fresh off the grill

items.rule: If the script claims charcoal fire, live grilling, or strong heat, the plan must show flame, red charcoal, turning, brushing, seasoning, smoke, or sizzle rather than only finished stills.
items.why_it_matters: The difference between barbecue and ordinary dining is live heat and visible craft. The viewer needs to feel that the grill is active now, not implied later.
execution_points: Prioritize grill-state shots, skewer turning, brushing oil or sauce, dry seasoning, and lift-off from the grill.
Connect heat shots to the next proof layer: table handoff, dipping, beer pairing, or the first bite.
If open flame cannot be shown directly, use charcoal glow, grill marks, steam, and fresh handoff shots as the downgraded proof route.
items.fallback_route: If grill access is limited, move to a hot-finished-product route and clearly avoid overclaiming live-fire visuals.
must_avoid: post-production flames pretending to be real fire
claims of live grilling with no process shots
recutting one skewer into multiple fake hero products

items.rule: If the plan wants to prove popularity, it must show real night-service signals such as storefront flow, occupied tables, outdoor seating, refills, add-on orders, or empty skewers on tables.
items.why_it_matters: Barbecue demand is judged heavily by late-night energy and repeat traffic. Viewers trust operational signals more than narration about how busy the shop is.
execution_points: Specify whether the shoot is at dusk, dinner peak, or late-night service, then match the shot list to that window.
Capture storefront movement, outdoor seating occupancy, servers bringing skewers and drinks, and table turnover evidence.
If the store is not busy that day, pivot to signature items, group-fit scenes, or comfort and space rather than forcing a crowd story.
required_evidence: service rhythm shots such as skewers, drinks, or refill moments
items.fallback_route: If peak traffic is unavailable, switch to a food-first or single-table route and explicitly mark the time-window limitation.
must_avoid: borrowing crowd footage from another venue
editing empty tables into fake peak-hour density
describing weekday flat traffic as festival-level business

items.rule: For fresh-cut meat, oysters, seafood, or made-to-order skewers, the storyboard should connect prep, grill, handoff, and bite in at least a three-step chain.
items.why_it_matters: Claims around freshness or live prep only feel credible when the viewer can follow the item through multiple real stages.
execution_points: Show item identity early through prep, labeling, or display before the grill stage.
Keep the same hero item consistent from prep to grill to table so the chain is easy to verify.
If back-of-house access is restricted, use front prep, pass station, or table-side handoff as the replacement route.
required_evidence: bite, dip, or table feedback shot
items.fallback_route: If the prep stage cannot be captured, preserve a clear grill-to-bite chain and note the missing upstream proof in dependencies.
must_avoid: mixing unrelated ingredient footage into one fake chain
using one food class to back another claim
over-grading color until doneness and freshness look unreliable

items.rule: The opening section should establish storefront, seating type, and venue position so viewers understand whether this is a street-side late-night shop, a mall grill bar, or a seafood stall.
items.why_it_matters: Barbecue decisions depend on who the place is for and how people sit there, not only on close-up food footage.
execution_points: For street-side stores, show sign, outdoor seating, and entry path. For mall stores, show floor signage, storefront, and seating relation.
If the shop has booths, private rooms, open-air seating, or a visible grill station, connect them in one readable route.
Use store-space shots to support the use case: after-work drinks, late-night social, family dinner, or seafood night-market hangout.
items.fallback_route: If the exterior cannot be filmed well, use sign, cashier, logo wall, and seating relation instead of pretending to have a stronger location footprint than reality.
must_avoid: all food close-ups with no store anchor
borrowing another store's interior as venue proof
cropping so tightly that store scale and type become misleading

items.rule: Skewer counts, combo trays, seafood plates, drinks, and value sets must match real menu availability and standard portioning rather than test plates or display-only arrangements.
items.why_it_matters: Barbecue buyers are sensitive to mismatch between what the video promises and what reaches the table. Portion credibility affects trust directly.
execution_points: Confirm what is normally on sale, what is seasonal, and what only appears in limited bundles or special windows.
If value or group-sharing is a core claim, use whole-table composition and clear quantity references rather than isolated beauty shots.
Separate single skewers, platters, seafood add-ons, drink combos, and fixed sets so the plan does not blur different offer types.
items.fallback_route: If a seafood or special item is only seasonal, label it clearly as a limited offer instead of a daily hero product.
must_avoid: using test or holiday platters as everyday proof
recutting one tray as multiple offer types
claiming value or quantity with no visible reference

items.rule: If comfort, low-smoke seating, or long-stay friendliness is part of the story, the plan needs visible proof of clean tables, grate changes, plate clearing, tissues, spacing, or ventilation.
items.why_it_matters: Barbecue can easily look greasy or uncomfortable. A credible plan balances smoke and appetite with cleanliness and staying power.
execution_points: Capture a clean table baseline before the first heavy food wave.
Use service details such as grate change, table reset, empty-skewer clearing, or tissue replenishment to prove care.
For indoor stores, include exhaust or spacing evidence. For outdoor stores, include comfort and airflow rather than only smoke.
required_evidence: clean table or seating baseline shot
items.fallback_route: If comfort proof is weak, do not lead on low-smoke or family-friendly claims. Shift back to food proof or late-night social proof.
must_avoid: treating excessive smoke as a pure visual asset
using grease and ash as appetite close-ups
ignoring nearby guest comfort when designing indoor shots

items.rule: If the script sells smooth late-night service, the plan should connect at least three of these stages: order, first skewers, drinks, refill, grate care, add-on order, or post-meal table evidence.
items.why_it_matters: A barbecue shop is experienced as a full table rhythm, not a single plated beauty moment. A connected chain makes the plan feel deliverable and real.
execution_points: Start from menu scan, drink arrival, or first skewers and then show how the session develops.
For social or group scenes, let multiple people share the table instead of repeating one hand with one skewer.
If the service chain is incomplete, declare the missing stages in dependencies rather than faking a full night arc.
items.fallback_route: If the full chain is not available, keep a shorter single-table experience route and surface the missing service nodes clearly.
must_avoid: cross-table edits pretending to be one continuous session
using another venue's diner interactions as proof
cutting so fast that the service logic becomes unreadable

items.rule: The plan must explicitly list shooting windows, grill access, outdoor seating permission, customer visibility rules, fire-safety boundaries, weather limits, and specific hero items needed for pickup shots.
items.why_it_matters: Barbecue filming depends on real operating windows, smoke, flame, table availability, and permissions. Missing one of those can break execution fast.
execution_points: Mark which shots happen before rush, during peak, during active grilling, or near close.
List the exact skewers, platters, seafood items, drinks, tables, and grill positions that must be available.
Add alternatives for no-face rules, no-fire closeups, rain, mall restrictions, or blocked outdoor seating.
items.fallback_route: If key dependencies fail, reduce ambition immediately to a food-first, single-table, space-intro, or pre-opening route.
must_avoid: assuming fire or outdoor seating is always available to film
assuming all customers can be shown clearly
designing a peak-service long chain with no dependency notes

items.rule: When assets are missing, downgrade honestly instead of inventing crowds, smoke, diners, or grill proof from external footage.
items.why_it_matters: The visualizer role is responsible for executable barbecue plans, not for manufacturing nightlife or appetite signals that the store cannot support.
execution_points: No crowd means a food-and-space route. One real table means a single-table experience route. Full peak footage unlocks a business-proof route.
Lower execution scores when proof layers are missing instead of preserving the script's original ambition.
Keep barbecue-specific anchors such as skewers, grill marks, beer, outdoor seating, or late-night timing even in downgraded routes.
items.fallback_route: Move the story to hero skewers, beer pairing, space explanation, tasting route, or pre-opening teaser rather than a fake hot-shop exploration video.
must_avoid: stock diners presented as repeat-business proof
rendered or staged trays presented as mature-store proof
execution scores higher than real shoot conditions justify
